1: Moses's first objection (hen lo ya'aminu li, "but they will not believe me") focuses on credibility, not capability. Aman (H539, believe/trust) — the same root as amen; his concern is that the nation will not extend covenantal trust to his claim of divine appointment. This is a practical objection, not theological rebellion: he has seen the bush but the elders have not; how does testimony without proof carry weight in a community that has been crushed into skepticism?
2: What is in your hand? — YHWH's response begins not with an argument but with an inventory of what is already present. The matteh (H4294, staff/rod) is the shepherd's basic tool — for guiding, defending, testing terrain. YHWH consistently works with what the servant already has rather than providing entirely new instruments. The question redirects attention from the impossible ("they won't believe me") to the available ("what do you already hold?").
3: Throw it on the ground — the staff becomes a nachash (H5175, serpent). The same word as the serpent of Eden (Gen 3:1); the wilderness is full of serpents (Num 21:6); the Egyptian deity Wadjet was depicted as a uraeus (cobra). In context, the sign has anti-Egyptian symbolic resonance: the serpent that threatens Israel (Egypt) can be grasped and controlled by the one who serves YHWH. Moses "ran from it" — appropriate fear, even from the instrument of his own wonder-working.
4: Grab it by the tail — contrary to safe snake-handling, which requires grasping just behind the head. The command requires trust: Moses must grasp the snake where it can still bite him, relying on YHWH's protection rather than his own technique. The serpent becomes a staff again (matteh, H4294) "in his palm" — the instrument of power is restored by obedience to the dangerous command.
5: The purpose statement for the signs: "so that they may believe that YHWH the God of their fathers — the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — appeared to you." The signs are epistemological tools, not mere spectacle; they function to create the emunah (covenantal trust) that Moses feared would be absent. The covenantal name "God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob" (cf. Exod 3:6) anchors the commission in the patriarchal covenant.
6: Put your hand in your cloak — the second sign: the hand emerges leprous (tsara'at, H6879). Leprosy in the OT was associated with divine judgment (Num 12:10, Miriam; 2 Kgs 5, Naaman; 2 Chr 26:19-20, Uzziah), but also with divine power to afflict and restore. The cloak (cheq, H2436, bosom/chest fold) is where valuables were kept — Moses puts his hand into the place of security and it emerges defiled; then back again and it is restored "like the rest of his flesh." The sign enacts the reversal of judgment.
7: The restoration of the leprous hand — "like the rest of his flesh" (ke-besaro) — demonstrates both the afflicting and healing power of the same God. The sign is not primarily about Egypt but about YHWH's absolute sovereignty over bodily integrity, which will later be demonstrated in the plagues (afflicting Egypt) and the wilderness healing (restoring Israel).
8: Three signs in escalating weight: the first (staff/serpent) may be dismissed as magic; the second (leprous hand) is harder to replicate; the third (Nile water→blood) touches the sacred heart of Egyptian religion (the Nile as Hapy, the inundation deity). The conditional structure — "if not the first, then the second; if not both, then the third" — grants Israel a graduated level of evidence before requiring faith, consistent with YHWH's pattern of meeting people where they are.
9: Water from the Nile becomes blood on dry ground — a miniature preview of the first plague (Exod 7:20). Ye'or (H2975, the Nile) is the Egyptian loanword for the great river, used throughout Exodus specifically for the Nile rather than the generic Hebrew nahar (river). The sign deliberately targets Egypt's most sacred resource.
10: The fourth objection — "I have never been eloquent (lo ish devarim anokhi)" — is the most specific: Moses is literally "not a man of words/speech." Devarim (H1697) is the plural of davar (word/matter), the same term used for "the ten commandments" (Deut 10:4, "ten words"). The qualification "neither in the past nor since you have spoken to me" acknowledges that even this divine encounter has not changed his speech capacity — he is describing an ongoing condition, not a temporary case of nerves.
11: Who gave human beings their mouths? — YHWH's response is a series of rhetorical questions asserting his creative sovereignty over all speech capacities: "who makes them deaf (cheresh, H2795) or mute (illem, H483)? who gives sight or makes blind (iver, H5787)?" The answer is implicit: "Is it not I, YHWH?" The argument: the Creator of speech has more capacity to enable speech than the speaker has to claim incapacity. This is not a dismissal of Moses's limitation but a reframing of where the power lies.
12: "I will be with your mouth (im-pikha, literally with your mouth) and will teach you what to say." The divine commitment is specific: not to remove Moses's limitation but to accompany it and work through it. Yara (H3384, teach/instruct) — the same root as Torah; YHWH will continuously instruct Moses in real time what to say. This is not a prepared script but ongoing divine direction.
13: Please send someone else — the fifth and final objection, most direct: shelach na be-yad tishlach, "send by the hand of [whoever] you will send." Moses does not name a substitute but gestures at anyone other than himself. This provokes YHWH's anger (vayichar af YHWH, H2734 + H639) — the only time in this extended commissioning that YHWH expresses anger. The objections have moved from legitimate concerns to outright reluctance.
14: Aaron the Levite — the tribal identification "the Levite" is unusual when speaking of one's own brother; it may signal Aaron's priestly role before the Sinai legislation or distinguish him from other Aarons. "He can speak well" (daber yedaber, H1696 doubled — an infinitive absolute construction meaning "he can surely/fluently speak"); the intensive form emphasizes genuine eloquence. "He is already on his way to meet you" — Aaron's movement toward Moses is already in progress, a sign that YHWH prepared the solution before Moses raised the final objection.
15: YHWH promises to instruct both Moses and Aaron simultaneously: "I will help you both speak and will teach you what to do." The partnership of two speakers — one receiving the revelation, one articulating it — will become the model for the prophet-priest collaboration throughout Israel's history. Moses as the prophet who receives and transmits; Aaron as the priest who speaks to the assembly.
16: "He will be as your mouth, and you will be as God to him" — ve-hayah hu yih'yeh-lekha le-feh ve-atah tih'yeh-lo le-Elohim. "As God to him" (le-Elohim, H430) is a remarkable designation: Moses functions as the divine representative to Aaron, who functions as his mouth to the people. The structure is: YHWH → Moses (revelation) → Aaron (articulation) → people (reception). This is the first explicit description of prophetic mediation as a chain of communication.
17: "The staff of God" (matteh ha-Elohim) — the shepherd's staff has been transformed by the commission: ordinary wood is now the instrument of divine signs. The naming of the staff as "God's staff" is the first of several renamings throughout Exodus that mark how ordinary objects are consecrated by covenant use.
18: Moses returns to Jethro and asks permission to leave — a notable act of covenantal courtesy toward his father-in-law. He does not reveal the full content of his divine commission but uses the more generic "to see whether my relatives are still alive." Yeter (Jethro's alternative name, H3503) appears here; the name Yitro (H3499) is used elsewhere — two forms of the same name, perhaps indicating two traditions merged in the final text.
19: YHWH's reassurance: "all those who wanted to kill you are dead" — referring to the Egyptians who sought Moses after the killing of the overseer (Exod 2:15). This addresses an unspoken fear Moses had not articulated in his five objections: the personal danger of returning. The phrase "all who sought your life are dead" reappears almost verbatim in 1 Kgs 19:10, 14 (Elijah's complaint) and Matt 2:20 (Joseph's dream about returning from Egypt), marking it as a standard form of divine reassurance.
20: Moses takes his wife Zipporah and sons (banav, H1121, his sons — more than one, though only one son is named previously) and begins the return journey. "The staff of God (matteh ha-Elohim) in his hand" — the phrase closes the bracket opened in v.17; Moses departs equipped as the divine agent.
21: "I will harden Pharaoh's heart" — ani achazzeq et-lev Par'oh (H2388, to harden/strengthen + H3820, heart). This is the first mention of the heart-hardening theme. Three different verbs are used throughout the plague narratives: chazaq (to strengthen/make stubborn, here), kabed (to make heavy, as a burden), and qashah (to make hard/severe). In the first five plagues Pharaoh hardens his own heart; in the last five YHWH hardens it. The theological tension (human responsibility vs. divine sovereignty) is part of the text's design, not its inconsistency.
22: "Israel is my firstborn son" — beni vekori Yisrael (H1121 + H1060, my son my firstborn). The covenant sonship of Israel is here declared officially for the first time to Pharaoh. In the ancient world, the firstborn son holds the highest status in the family — rights, inheritance, authority. YHWH's designation of a nation of slaves as his firstborn son is the antithesis of their social status; covenantal reality inverts empirical standing.
23: The covenant logic of the tenth plague is stated before the plagues begin: "Let my son go... you refused... so I will kill your firstborn." The structure is covenantal: Pharaoh's refusal to release YHWH's firstborn will be repaid through the loss of Egypt's firstborn. This is not arbitrary retaliation but covenantal reciprocity — what is done to YHWH's son will be done to the oppressor's son.
24: The lodging-place attack — "the LORD met him and sought to kill him" (vayevaqesh hamito). One of the most syntactically compressed and theologically mysterious passages in the Pentateuch. The subject of "kill him" is ambiguous — it could refer to Moses, though the traditional reading (and what makes Zipporah's response intelligible) assumes it is Moses. The divine agent is understood as the "angel of YHWH" (cf. Gen 32:24-30, Jacob's night wrestling). The crisis appears to be circumcision: Moses had not circumcised his son born in Midian, thereby leaving the covenant mark incomplete.
25: Zipporah took a flint knife and cut off her son's foreskin — she acts as the covenant agent when Moses cannot. Tzur (H6697, flint) — stone knives were used for circumcision even when metal was available (Josh 5:2-3), preserving the archaic ritual. She "touched his feet (raglayv, H7272)" — in Hebrew, "feet" can be a euphemism for genitals (e.g., Ruth 3:7). The blood of circumcision (substituting for Moses's blood) averts the death threat. Bridegroom of blood (chatan damim, H2860 + H1818) — the Aramaic cognate of chatan (bridegroom) means "circumcised one"; the phrase may encode the covenant link between circumcision and marriage-covenant identity.
26: "The LORD let him alone" — the crisis passes with the circumcision completed. The parenthetical note "(she said 'bridegroom of blood' in reference to circumcision)" is the narrator's gloss explaining the cryptic phrase. The passage is preserved in its irreducible difficulty — an encounter with divine judgment that is averted not by explanation but by covenant action (blood).
27: YHWH instructs Aaron to go into the wilderness to meet Moses; Aaron meets him "at the mountain of God" — Horeb, where the bush appeared. The reunion is divinely staged: the two brothers meet at the very site of Moses's commissioning, so that Aaron enters the commission-context before beginning his ministry. Vayishaq lo (he kissed him) — the covenant greeting, as between Jacob and Esau (Gen 33:4) and the father and the prodigal son (Luke 15:20).
28: Moses "told Aaron everything YHWH had commanded him to say and all the signs he had commanded." The transfer of the commission from Moses to Aaron is total and exact — Aaron is fully briefed, including the miracle-signs. The chain of communication (YHWH → Moses → Aaron, as described in 4:16) now operates for the first time.
29: Moses and Aaron assemble all the elders of Israel — a formal national gathering. The elders (zeqenim, H2205) are the representative heads of Israel's clan-based governance structure; convening them is equivalent to convening Israel's leadership in total.
30: Aaron speaks all the words and performs all the signs — precisely as the communication chain of vv.15-16 prescribed. The text is careful to have Aaron do both the speaking and the demonstrating; Moses is in the background, the unseen source. This division of labor will characterize their relationship throughout the Exodus narrative.
31: They believed, and bowed their heads in worship — vaya'aminu (H539, they believed/trusted) and vayishtachavu (H7812, they bowed down/worshipped). The community's response combines cognitive trust (aman) and physical reverence (prostration). The trigger for the worship: "YHWH was concerned about them (paqad, H6485 — visited/attended to) and had seen their misery (ra'ah et-aniyam)." The knowledge that they had been seen and cared for — not forgotten — produces worship.
